goal: Build v2 artifact #4, tools/heldout-suite/ — the deterministic held-out-suite lifecycle CLI (materialize an authoring workspace outside the repo, validate a suite against base under the fails-on-base policy, seal it into a spec-hash-bound manifest with a deny-fragment) plus the authoring role contract (ROLE.md) that any fresh-context agent can execute. This is design D2/D11 as a standalone artifact: the graded tests' lifecycle, with agent spawning explicitly out of scope (that is artifact #5's composition concern).
non_goals: No model/agent spawning — the headless-spawn machinery and its vendor-build probing land in artifact #5, once, for both roles.

No boundary-verification probe (spawning an implementer-configured process to prove the suite unreadable) — declined HERE to preserve decision 1's separation of concerns, not rejected forever; if wanted later it is its own standalone artifact composing through the deny-fragment file.
No replay/leakage accounting — the M1/M2 budget is the composer's ledger concern; this artifact only gives replays a stable identity to account against.
constraints: Pure stdlib Python 3; no imports from any other artifact in this repo (R5/D15).
materialize refuses an --out that resolves to a path inside the target repo (the v1 in-repo vault rode git history into worker worktrees).
Sealed suites are never silently overwritten — retirement moves them aside with a timestamp, preserving evidence.
Landing goes branch -> merge-gate run + verify -> --ff-only merge (dogfood), with the gate report appended to docs/research/internal/v2-ledger.jsonl.

decisions.q: Scope cut: is #4 the end-to-end test-author (including headless claude -p authoring spawn), or the deterministic suite lifecycle with the authoring agent supplied by composition?
decisions.a: Deterministic lifecycle only (materialize/validate/seal + ROLE.md). Spawning is #5's concern, where the headless-contract probing happens once for both implementer and test-author roles. This is also a clean separation of concerns with substitutability value: launching headless AI workers is the piece most likely to change shape entirely (headless tools other than Claude Code are plausible launchers later), and keeping it out of this artifact means that evolution never touches the suite lifecycle.

decisions.q: Isolation posture: where does the suite live and who enforces that the implementer cannot read it?
decisions.a: Outside the repo (CLI refuses in-repo --out as a sound check). The boundary itself is owned by whatever spawns the implementer (OS/deny config; deny rules have absolute precedence and resolve symlinks per distilled section 4, vendor-build decay). seal emits a ready-to-merge deny-fragment file so the composer wires the boundary from a file contract. The declined stronger option — this artifact itself proving the code-writer is blocked — is not impossible; it would cost decision 1's separation of concerns by pulling launch machinery back in. If wanted later it becomes its own standalone boundary-probe artifact composing through the same deny-fragment file.

decisions.q: Fails-on-base policy: how strict is validate about the suite's teeth?
decisions.a: At least one test must fail against base; the manifest records the exact failed/passed split. Base-passing tests are legitimate regression guards. ImportError on not-yet-existing modules counts as failing. Necessary-not-sufficient, stated plainly in the contract.

decisions.q: Sealing and re-authoring: when may a suite be re-authored?
decisions.a: The seal is a record file storing three fingerprints together — a hash of every test file, a hash of the spec the tests were authored from, and the repository commit they were validated against — so any later change to any of them is mechanically detectable. Re-authoring only on spec change (new spec hash; old suite retired, kept as evidence). Base moved with spec unchanged: re-run validate, never re-author. Disputes: THE SPEC IS THE ARBITER — a test is bad precisely when it asserts something the spec does not require; if the spec cannot arbitrate, the spec is the defect (underspecified) and amending it is the fix. Two doors: (1) spec clarification/amendment -> new hash -> legitimate re-author; (2) a human judges the test wrong and edits the suite directly, re-sealing with an adjudication record (by/note/ts) in the manifest — humans are outside the blindness constraint; model roles are not. Stoppage scope while a dispute is open (the disputed task and its dependents block; independent tasks continue) and its escalation choreography belong to the execution loop's future design; this artifact contributes the two doors and the tamper-evident record.

decisions.q: Authoring role input scope: what does the test-author get to see?
decisions.a: This task's full plan entry plus the plan-level preamble (goal, non_goals, constraints, decisions) plus the base checkout. Not other tasks' specs — suites test their own task's contract at its declared seams; seam discipline, not secrecy, is the reason pre-implementation.

decisions.q: Cross-task integration testing: where does it live, given each suite sees only its own task?
decisions.a: As an explicit integration task in the same plan whenever the seams warrant it — a first-class task whose spec describes the cross-task contract, depending on the tasks it integrates, with its own held-out suite authored from that spec; this tool treats it like any other task. Never a separate interview or effort, and never an accidental byproduct. For this plan's three sequential tasks, the artifact's own end-to-end test suite (every task's check) covers the integrated materialize->author->validate->seal flow; a separate integration task would be padding.

tasks.title: Workspace contract + materialize subcommand
tasks.spec: Create tools/heldout-suite/heldout.py with the house CLI conventions (argparse subcommands; exit 0 ok / 1 refusal or policy failure / 2 usage-IO error; JSON to stdout). Subcommand: `materialize --plan PLAN.json --task TASK_ID --repo REPO --base REF --out DIR`. Behavior: (1) resolve --out to a real path and refuse (exit 1) if it lies inside REPO's toplevel — the sound in-repo check; (2) load the plan (contract 1), find TASK_ID, and write the authoring workspace at OUT/TASK_ID/: authoring/task.json holding exactly the interview-scoped inputs — the task's full entry plus plan-level goal, non_goals, constraints, decisions (never other tasks) — plus base ref and its resolved sha from REPO; (3) create empty suite/ for the author's test files and copy nothing else; (4) write authoring/AUTHORING.md, a generated pointer stating: what is in task.json, that tests go in suite/ as test_*.py (stdlib unittest), that the suite will be judged by `validate` against a clean base checkout under the fails-on-base policy, and that the full role instructions are ROLE.md in the artifact directory; (5) refuse (exit 1) if OUT/TASK_ID already exists — re-materialization of a sealed workspace is the retire flow in validate-seal, not an overwrite. Tests in tools/heldout-suite/test_heldout.py: in-repo --out refusal (incl. symlink into the repo), correct task.json extraction (preamble present, sibling tasks absent), base sha recorded, existing-workspace refusal.

tasks.title: validate (fails-on-base) + seal (manifest + deny-fragment) + retire
tasks.spec: Add to tools/heldout-suite/heldout.py: `validate --workspace OUT/TASK_ID --repo REPO` — creates a throwaway detached git worktree of the workspace's recorded base sha (same pattern as merge-gate but re-implemented here, no cross-artifact imports), runs the suite (python3 -m unittest discover -s suite/ with cwd and sys.path arranged so the suite imports the checked-out code), and applies the policy: runner must complete and report; at least one test must fail; record {failed, passed, errored} where ImportError-style errors count as failures-on-base. Emits a validation record JSON into the workspace (validation.json with base_sha, split, ts). Always removes the worktree. `seal --workspace OUT/TASK_ID` — re-runs validate internally, then writes manifest.json {contract: 1, task_id, spec_hash: sha256 of canonical authoring/task.json, base_sha, run: the exact suite invocation, files: sorted suite file list with sha256s, fails_on_base: the split, sealed_at} and deny-fragment.json {deny_read: [absolute suite paths]} — the ready-to-merge file for an implementer-spawner's settings. seal refuses if the workspace is already sealed unless --retire is passed, which moves the existing suite/ + manifest.json to retired/<ts>/ before re-sealing (evidence preserved, decisions[3]). seal also accepts --adjudicated-by NAME with --adjudication-note TEXT, recording manifest.adjudication {by, note, ts} — the on-the-record path for a human-edited suite (decisions[3]'s second door); these flags do not bypass --retire or validation. Tests: all-pass suite refuses seal; one-failing suite seals with correct split; ImportError counts as failing; spec-hash changes when task.json changes; retire flow preserves the old suite; adjudicated re-seal records the adjudication block; deny-fragment paths are absolute and correct; worktree cleanup verified.

tasks.title: ROLE.md authoring contract + artifact README + repo wiring
tasks.spec: Write tools/heldout-suite/ROLE.md — the authoring agent's instructions, the role-contract analogue of spec-interview's SKILL.md: you receive an authoring workspace (task.json + base checkout access) and nothing else; write adversarial tests from the SPEC, not from any implementation (none exists); test this task's contract at its declared seams (requires/provides), edge and failure behavior named in the spec, and regression guards for base behavior the change must preserve; put tests in suite/ as stdlib-unittest test_*.py; you never assert your suite is good — `validate` and the seal decide; you never read outside the workspace and base checkout. Write tools/heldout-suite/README.md in the house contract style: one-thing-well statement (D2/D11), the three verbs with exit codes, the manifest and deny-fragment schemas, guarantees (in-repo refusal, fails-on-base policy verbatim from decisions[2], seal binding, retire-preserves-evidence), non-goals (verbatim the plan's), composition examples (gate --check wiring of a sealed suite's run command; deny-fragment merge into a spawner's settings; ledger append of a seal record), the R2 deletion criterion (judged by escapes caught that visible checks missed — the P3v2-5 precedent), and the T11 note. Add the root README.md layout row for tools/heldout-suite/ (v2 artifact #4). Run the corpus link guard.
